Clarity.justification: The paper is exceptionally well-structured with clear sections (Abstract, Introduction, Related Work, Method, Results, Discussion, Conclusion). Ideas are articulated logically, with precise definitions of C_sem, partner-specificity hierarchy, and sub-hypotheses. Tables summarize tier structures and results; figures (e.g., partner-specificity bars, directional asymmetry heatmap) enhance readability. Minor areas for improvement include occasional dense statistical descriptions, but overall coherence is high.

Novelty.justification: The work extends Communication Accommodation Theory to semantic embeddings (SBERT cosine) in human-AI RLHF conversations, introducing C_sem with random/KNN controls and testing monotonicity across three tiers. It falsifies within-conversation mechanisms (reversed delta in h-m3, null mediation in h-m4). While building on prior CAT, RLHF datasets, and style-adaptation studies, the cross-tier population-level analysis and bidirectional asymmetry focus represent a notable but not groundbreaking advance.

Soundness.justification: Methods are appropriate and rigorous: L2-normalized SBERT embeddings, three-level controls (actual > KNN > random), bootstrap CIs, Mann-Whitney, Jonckheere-Terpstra, and OLS mediation with HC3 SE. Results replicate across three SBERT architectures; code implements residualization, topic controls (n_jobs=1), and per-tier C_sem computation. Limitations (cross-sectional design, SBERT topic-style entanglement) are explicitly acknowledged. Minor weakness: IPW correction details could be expanded for full reproducibility.

Significance.justification: Addresses an important problem in bidirectional human-AI alignment by showing RLHF tier quality correlates with human semantic behavior at population level rather than via within-conversation perception. Implications for RLHF evaluation (incorporating human-side patterns) and warnings about chosen/rejected annotations are valuable. Impact is solid for HCI/NLP venues but tempered by incremental nature and cross-sectional constraints precluding causal claims.

strengths: Novel C_sem metric with strong controls and replication across models
Clear falsification of mechanism hypotheses with statistical rigor
High reproducibility via provided code and detailed experimental logs
weaknesses: Cross-sectional design limits causal interpretation of tier-accommodation link
SBERT embeddings entangle topical and stylistic components despite KNN controls
